In addition, a bettō-ji known as Enman-ji was established. The enshrined deity is the son of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|de|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル}}, and the half-brother of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]. He is said to have followed [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] in the {{ill|Campaigns against the Three Han|de|Drei-Han-Feldzüge|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Campaigns against the Three Han|qq=}}, later remaining in the area and being commissioned to guard the western regions, known as {{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|de|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|ja|十城別命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})). The ground shrine is said to have repurposed an armory established by the king, while the offshore shrine is believed to mark his tomb. In the Divine Name List of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engi-shiki]], within [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]], it is listed as one of the four {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}; it is the only shrine of its kind in Nagasaki Prefecture, excluding the Iki and Tsushima regions, and is the oldest known shrine. It has long garnered widespread reverence from the {{ill|Matsuura Faction|de|Matsuura Fraktion|ja|松浦党|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Faction|qq=}} and local fishermen. The current center of worship is the middle shrine. Originally, the shrine was housed in a small structure at the foot of Shijiki Mountain’s summit, but it was relocated to the mid-slope in [[:en:Eiroku|Yōroku]] 2 ([[:en:1559|1559]]). Furthermore, in [[:en:1961|1961]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 36), it was moved to its present location, which occupies the former site of Enman-ji—a bettō-ji abolished in the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|de|Trennung von Shinto und Buddhismus Dekret|ja|神仏分離令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離}}. The former shrine grounds were designated as a historical site of Nagasaki Prefecture in [[:en:1974|1974]] (Showa 49), under the name "Former Shikinaisha Shijiki Shrine Site." == Footnotes == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{commonscat|Shijiki-jinja}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{Shinto stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Authority control}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[de:Shijiki Shrine]] [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] ==Merged second translation== == Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) == '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志々伎神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Noko Town|ja|野子町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noko Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also written as '''Shishiki Shrine''' or '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志志伎神社 or 志自伎神社). It is listed as a '''small-scale''' {{ill|Shikinai shrine|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinai shrine|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, and under the '''modern shrine Ranking system''', it held the status of a '''former prefectural shrine'''. Several branch shrines bearing the name '''Shijiki''', '''Shishiki''', or '''Shijiki Shrine''' exist throughout Kyushu. As the '''largest of Nagasaki's Shikinaisha''', it has played a significant historical role. The '''sacred sumo ritual''' practiced here is believed to be an early form of '''sumo wrestling'''[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 Shijiki Shrine]. Kadokawa Japan Place Name Encyclopedia, JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3, Nasubi Historical Archive. Archived from [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html the original] on May 18, 2003.. The '''Hirado Jangara Dance''' (平戸のジャンガラ), a local folk performance seen across '''Hirado Island''', is said to have originated from this shrine. == Overview == The founding date of '''Shijiki Shrine''' is uncertain, though some sources suggest it dates back to the '''5th century'''. The shrine’s '''sacred domain''' is centered on '''Mount Shijiki''' (志々伎山), located near the '''southern tip of Hirado Island'''. Traditionally, the shrine was divided into four main sanctuaries: * '''Upper Shrine (Kami-no-Miya)''': Located at the '''summit''' of Mount Shijiki. * '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''': Located '''midway up the mountain'''. * '''Lower Shrine (Chi-no-Miya)''': Located at '''Miyanoura''', a coastal fishing village. * '''Offshore Shrine (Oki-no-Miya)''': Located on '''Okinoshima''', an island off the coast. Additionally, the '''Enman-ji Temple''' (円満寺) historically served as the shrine’s '''bettō-ji''' (shrine-temple complex before the Meiji-era Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''deity enshrined''' here is '''Tokowake-no-Ō''' (十城別王), also known as '''Tokowake-no-Mikoto''' (十城別命), the '''son of Yamato Takeru''' and the '''half-brother of Emperor Chūai'''. He is believed to have '''accompanied Empress Jingū on her conquest of Korea''' and was later '''assigned to defend Western Japan'''. According to legend: * '''Chi-no-Miya''' (Lower Shrine) was originally a '''military weapons storehouse''' established by Tokowake-no-Ō. * '''Oki-no-Miya''' (Offshore Shrine) is said to '''house his tomb'''. Shijiki Shrine is listed in the '''Engishiki''' (延喜式) as '''one of only four Shikinaisha''' in '''Hizen Province'''. Excluding the '''Iki and Tsushima regions''', it is the '''only Engishiki shrine in modern Nagasaki Prefecture'''. Historically, it attracted strong patronage from the '''Matsura clan''' (松浦党) and the surrounding '''maritime communities'''. Today, the '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''' is the primary place of worship. The shrine originally '''stood at the mountain’s peak''', but in '''1559 (Eiroku 2)''', it was moved '''midway down the mountain'''. In '''1961 (Shōwa 36)''', it was '''relocated again''' to the '''former site of Enman-ji Temple''', which had been abolished following the '''Meiji-era Shinbutsu Bunri''' (Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''original shrine site''' was designated a '''Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1974 (Shōwa 49)''' as the '''"Former Site of the Shikinai Shrine Shijiki Shrine"'''. == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] }} [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shikizuki Shrine]] [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirado, Nagasaki]] [[Category:History of Hirado City]] {{Commons category|Shijiki-jinja}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11490808]] Wikidata name: Shijiki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| |Name=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) |image=File:Shijiki-jinja, Hirado 202405-1.jpg |Location=[[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado City]] Nogomachi 251 |Coordinates={{Wiki Coordinates Two-Level DMS|33|10|44.15|N|129|22|08.55|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社)}} |Enshrined deity={{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |Shrine Rank=Shikinaisha (small)
Former Prefectural Shrine |Founded=Unknown |Main Hall= |Alternate name= |Temple sites, etc.= |Annual festival= |Rituals= |Map={{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|marker=place-of-worship}}
{{Right|{{Location map|Japan Nagasaki#Japan|label=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=Location of Shijiki Shrine}}}} |prefecture={{ill|Hirado|qid=Q328601}} |coordinates={{Coord|33.178930555556|N|129.36904166667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Shijiki Shrine''' (Shijikijinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Nogomachi, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado City]] in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also referred to as Shijiki Shrine or Shiji Shrine. It holds the status of a {{ill|Engi-shiki Divine Registry|de|Engi-shiki Göttliche Namensliste|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|lt=Small Shikinaisha|lt_ja=式内小社|lt_de=Kleine Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi-shiki Divine Registry|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and a [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Old Shrine Rank]] shrine, designating it as a prefectural shrine. Branch shrines exist throughout Kyushu under the names Shijiki, Shishiki, and Shiji, making it the largest among prefectural Shikinaisha. Ancient deity sumo, preserved here, is said to be the precursor to modern [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]].[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 志々伎神社]. 角川日本地名大辞典.JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3. Nasubi History Museum. As of May 18, 2003, the [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Original]{{リンク切れ|date=2022年2月}} archive The Jangara, performed throughout Hirado Island, also spread from this shrine. == Overview == It is said that the shrine was founded around the 5th century, though the exact date remains unknown. The sacred precinct centers on {{ill|Shijiki Mountain|de|Shijiki Berg|ja|志々伎山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijiki Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Shijiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490806}} (with an elevation of 347.2 [[:en:Metre|meter]]) near the southern tip of [[:en:Hirado Island|Hirado Island]]. In ancient times, the upper shrine was located at the mountain summit, the middle shrine on the mid-slope, and the ground shrine (also known as the lower shrine or Hentomiyashiro) was situated in the fishing village of Miyano-ura at the foot, while an offshore shrine was placed on an islet off Miyano-ura. In addition, a bettō-ji known as Enman-ji was established. The enshrined deity is the son of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|de|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|1=Yamato Takeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, and the half-brother of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]. He is said to have followed [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] in the {{ill|Campaigns against the Three Han|de|Drei-Han-Feldzüge|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Campaigns against the Three Han|qq=|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}}, later remaining in the area and being commissioned to guard the western regions, known as {{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|de|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|ja|十城別命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})). The ground shrine is said to have repurposed an armory established by the king, while the offshore shrine is believed to mark his tomb. In the Divine Name List of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engi-shiki]], within [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]], it is listed as one of the four {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}; it is the only shrine of its kind in Nagasaki Prefecture, excluding the Iki and Tsushima regions, and is the oldest known shrine. It has long garnered widespread reverence from the {{ill|Matsuura Faction|de|Matsuura Fraktion|ja|松浦党|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Faction|qq=|1=Matsura-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17564485}} and local fishermen. The current center of worship is the middle shrine. Originally, the shrine was housed in a small structure at the foot of Shijiki Mountain’s summit, but it was relocated to the mid-slope in [[:en:Eiroku|Yōroku]] 2 ([[:en:1559|1559]]). Furthermore, in [[:en:1961|1961]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 36), it was moved to its present location, which occupies the former site of Enman-ji—a bettō-ji abolished in the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|de|Trennung von Shinto und Buddhismus Dekret|ja|神仏分離令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}. The former shrine grounds were designated as a historical site of Nagasaki Prefecture in [[:en:1974|1974]] (Showa 49), under the name "Former Shikinaisha Shijiki Shrine Site." == Footnotes == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{commonscat|Shijiki-jinja}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{Shinto stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Authority control}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[de:Shijiki Shrine]] [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] ==Merged second translation== == Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) == '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志々伎神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Noko Town|ja|野子町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noko Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also written as '''Shishiki Shrine''' or '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志志伎神社 or 志自伎神社). It is listed as a '''small-scale''' {{ill|Shikinai shrine|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinai shrine|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, and under the '''modern shrine Ranking system''', it held the status of a '''former prefectural shrine'''. Several branch shrines bearing the name '''Shijiki''', '''Shishiki''', or '''Shijiki Shrine''' exist throughout Kyushu. As the '''largest of Nagasaki's Shikinaisha''', it has played a significant historical role. The '''sacred sumo ritual''' practiced here is believed to be an early form of '''sumo wrestling'''[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 Shijiki Shrine]. Kadokawa Japan Place Name Encyclopedia, JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3, Nasubi Historical Archive. Archived from [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html the original] on May 18, 2003.. The '''Hirado Jangara Dance''' (平戸のジャンガラ), a local folk performance seen across '''Hirado Island''', is said to have originated from this shrine. == Overview == The founding date of '''Shijiki Shrine''' is uncertain, though some sources suggest it dates back to the '''5th century'''. The shrine’s '''sacred domain''' is centered on '''Mount Shijiki''' (志々伎山), located near the '''southern tip of Hirado Island'''. Traditionally, the shrine was divided into four main sanctuaries: * '''Upper Shrine (Kami-no-Miya)''': Located at the '''summit''' of Mount Shijiki. * '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''': Located '''midway up the mountain'''. * '''Lower Shrine (Chi-no-Miya)''': Located at '''Miyanoura''', a coastal fishing village. * '''Offshore Shrine (Oki-no-Miya)''': Located on '''Okinoshima''', an island off the coast. Additionally, the '''Enman-ji Temple''' (円満寺) historically served as the shrine’s '''bettō-ji''' (shrine-temple complex before the Meiji-era Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''deity enshrined''' here is '''Tokowake-no-Ō''' (十城別王), also known as '''Tokowake-no-Mikoto''' (十城別命), the '''son of Yamato Takeru''' and the '''half-brother of Emperor Chūai'''. He is believed to have '''accompanied Empress Jingū on her conquest of Korea''' and was later '''assigned to defend Western Japan'''. According to legend: * '''Chi-no-Miya''' (Lower Shrine) was originally a '''military weapons storehouse''' established by Tokowake-no-Ō. * '''Oki-no-Miya''' (Offshore Shrine) is said to '''house his tomb'''. Shijiki Shrine is listed in the '''Engishiki''' (延喜式) as '''one of only four Shikinaisha''' in '''Hizen Province'''. Excluding the '''Iki and Tsushima regions''', it is the '''only Engishiki shrine in modern Nagasaki Prefecture'''. Historically, it attracted strong patronage from the '''Matsura clan''' (松浦党) and the surrounding '''maritime communities'''. Today, the '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''' is the primary place of worship. The shrine originally '''stood at the mountain’s peak''', but in '''1559 (Eiroku 2)''', it was moved '''midway down the mountain'''. In '''1961 (Shōwa 36)''', it was '''relocated again''' to the '''former site of Enman-ji Temple''', which had been abolished following the '''Meiji-era Shinbutsu Bunri''' (Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''original shrine site''' was designated a '''Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1974 (Shōwa 49)''' as the '''"Former Site of the Shikinai Shrine Shijiki Shrine"'''. == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] }} [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shikizuki Shrine]] [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirado, Nagasaki]] [[Category:History of Hirado City]] {{Commons category|Shijiki-jinja}}

{{Right|{{Location map|Japan Nagasaki#Japan|label=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=Location of Shijiki Shrine}}}} |prefecture={{ill|Hirado|qid=Q328601}} |coordinates={{Coord|33.178930555556|N|129.36904166667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Shijiki Shrine''' (Shijikijinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Nogomachi, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado City]] in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also referred to as Shijiki Shrine or Shiji Shrine. It holds the status of a {{ill|Engi-shiki Divine Registry|de|Engi-shiki Göttliche Namensliste|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|lt=Small Shikinaisha|lt_ja=式内小社|lt_de=Kleine Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi-shiki Divine Registry|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and a [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Old Shrine Rank]] shrine, designating it as a prefectural shrine. Branch shrines exist throughout Kyushu under the names Shijiki, Shishiki, and Shiji, making it the largest among prefectural Shikinaisha. Ancient deity sumo, preserved here, is said to be the precursor to modern [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]].[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 志々伎神社]. 角川日本地名大辞典.JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3. Nasubi History Museum. As of May 18, 2003, the [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Original]{{リンク切れ|date=2022年2月}} archive The Jangara, performed throughout Hirado Island, also spread from this shrine. == Overview == It is said that the shrine was founded around the 5th century, though the exact date remains unknown. The sacred precinct centers on {{ill|Shijiki Mountain|de|Shijiki Berg|ja|志々伎山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijiki Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Shijiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490806}} (with an elevation of 347.2 [[:en:Metre|meter]]) near the southern tip of [[:en:Hirado Island|Hirado Island]]. In ancient times, the upper shrine was located at the mountain summit, the middle shrine on the mid-slope, and the ground shrine (also known as the lower shrine or Hentomiyashiro) was situated in the fishing village of Miyano-ura at the foot, while an offshore shrine was placed on an islet off Miyano-ura. In addition, a bettō-ji known as Enman-ji was established. The enshrined deity is the son of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|de|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|1=Yamato Takeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, and the half-brother of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]. He is said to have followed [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] in the {{ill|Campaigns against the Three Han|de|Drei-Han-Feldzüge|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Campaigns against the Three Han|qq=|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}}, later remaining in the area and being commissioned to guard the western regions, known as {{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|de|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|ja|十城別命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})). The ground shrine is said to have repurposed an armory established by the king, while the offshore shrine is believed to mark his tomb. In the Divine Name List of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engi-shiki]], within [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]], it is listed as one of the four {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}; it is the only shrine of its kind in Nagasaki Prefecture, excluding the Iki and Tsushima regions, and is the oldest known shrine. It has long garnered widespread reverence from the {{ill|Matsuura Faction|de|Matsuura Fraktion|ja|松浦党|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Faction|qq=|1=Matsura-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17564485}} and local fishermen. The current center of worship is the middle shrine. Originally, the shrine was housed in a small structure at the foot of Shijiki Mountain’s summit, but it was relocated to the mid-slope in [[:en:Eiroku|Yōroku]] 2 ([[:en:1559|1559]]). Furthermore, in [[:en:1961|1961]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 36), it was moved to its present location, which occupies the former site of Enman-ji—a bettō-ji abolished in the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|de|Trennung von Shinto und Buddhismus Dekret|ja|神仏分離令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}. The former shrine grounds were designated as a historical site of Nagasaki Prefecture in [[:en:1974|1974]] (Showa 49), under the name "Former Shikinaisha Shijiki Shrine Site." == Footnotes == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{commonscat|Shijiki-jinja}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{Shinto stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Authority control}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[de:Shijiki Shrine]] [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] ==Merged second translation== == Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) == '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志々伎神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Noko Town|ja|野子町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noko Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also written as '''Shishiki Shrine''' or '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志志伎神社 or 志自伎神社). It is listed as a '''small-scale''' {{ill|Shikinai shrine|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinai shrine|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, and under the '''modern shrine Ranking system''', it held the status of a '''former prefectural shrine'''. Several branch shrines bearing the name '''Shijiki''', '''Shishiki''', or '''Shijiki Shrine''' exist throughout Kyushu. As the '''largest of Nagasaki's Shikinaisha''', it has played a significant historical role. The '''sacred sumo ritual''' practiced here is believed to be an early form of '''sumo wrestling'''[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 Shijiki Shrine]. Kadokawa Japan Place Name Encyclopedia, JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3, Nasubi Historical Archive. Archived from [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html the original] on May 18, 2003.. The '''Hirado Jangara Dance''' (平戸のジャンガラ), a local folk performance seen across '''Hirado Island''', is said to have originated from this shrine. == Overview == The founding date of '''Shijiki Shrine''' is uncertain, though some sources suggest it dates back to the '''5th century'''. The shrine’s '''sacred domain''' is centered on '''Mount Shijiki''' (志々伎山), located near the '''southern tip of Hirado Island'''. Traditionally, the shrine was divided into four main sanctuaries: * '''Upper Shrine (Kami-no-Miya)''': Located at the '''summit''' of Mount Shijiki. * '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''': Located '''midway up the mountain'''. * '''Lower Shrine (Chi-no-Miya)''': Located at '''Miyanoura''', a coastal fishing village. * '''Offshore Shrine (Oki-no-Miya)''': Located on '''Okinoshima''', an island off the coast. Additionally, the '''Enman-ji Temple''' (円満寺) historically served as the shrine’s '''bettō-ji''' (shrine-temple complex before the Meiji-era Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''deity enshrined''' here is '''Tokowake-no-Ō''' (十城別王), also known as '''Tokowake-no-Mikoto''' (十城別命), the '''son of Yamato Takeru''' and the '''half-brother of Emperor Chūai'''. He is believed to have '''accompanied Empress Jingū on her conquest of Korea''' and was later '''assigned to defend Western Japan'''. According to legend: * '''Chi-no-Miya''' (Lower Shrine) was originally a '''military weapons storehouse''' established by Tokowake-no-Ō. * '''Oki-no-Miya''' (Offshore Shrine) is said to '''house his tomb'''. Shijiki Shrine is listed in the '''Engishiki''' (延喜式) as '''one of only four Shikinaisha''' in '''Hizen Province'''. Excluding the '''Iki and Tsushima regions''', it is the '''only Engishiki shrine in modern Nagasaki Prefecture'''. Historically, it attracted strong patronage from the '''Matsura clan''' (松浦党) and the surrounding '''maritime communities'''. Today, the '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''' is the primary place of worship. The shrine originally '''stood at the mountain’s peak''', but in '''1559 (Eiroku 2)''', it was moved '''midway down the mountain'''. In '''1961 (Shōwa 36)''', it was '''relocated again''' to the '''former site of Enman-ji Temple''', which had been abolished following the '''Meiji-era Shinbutsu Bunri''' (Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''original shrine site''' was designated a '''Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1974 (Shōwa 49)''' as the '''"Former Site of the Shikinai Shrine Shijiki Shrine"'''. == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] }} [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shikizuki Shrine]] [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirado, Nagasaki]] [[Category:History of Hirado City]] {{Commons category|Shijiki-jinja}} [[Category:qq]]

The shrine originally '''stood at the mountain’s peak''', but in '''1559 (Eiroku 2)''', it was moved '''midway down the mountain'''. In '''1961 (Shōwa 36)''', it was '''relocated again''' to the '''former site of Enman-ji Temple''', which had been abolished following the '''Meiji-era Shinbutsu Bunri''' (Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''original shrine site''' was designated a '''Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1974 (Shōwa 49)''' as the '''"Former Site of the Shikinai Shrine Shijiki Shrine"'''. == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] }} [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shikizuki Shrine]] [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirado, Nagasaki]] [[Category:History of Hirado City]] {{Commons category|Shijiki-jinja}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11490808]] Wikidata name: Shijiki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| |Name=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) |image=File:Shijiki-jinja, Hirado 202405-1.jpg |Location=[[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado City]] Nogomachi 251 |Coordinates={{Wiki Coordinates Two-Level DMS|33|10|44.15|N|129|22|08.55|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社)}} |Enshrined deity={{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |Shrine Rank=Shikinaisha (small)
Former Prefectural Shrine |Founded=Unknown |Main Hall= |Alternate name= |Temple sites, etc.= |Annual festival= |Rituals= |Map={{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|marker=place-of-worship}}
{{Right|{{Location map|Japan Nagasaki#Japan|label=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=Location of Shijiki Shrine}}}} |prefecture={{ill|Hirado|qid=Q328601}} |coordinates={{Coord|33.178930555556|N|129.36904166667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Shijiki Shrine''' (Shijikijinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Nogomachi, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado City]] in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also referred to as Shijiki Shrine or Shiji Shrine. It holds the status of a {{ill|Engi-shiki Divine Registry|de|Engi-shiki Göttliche Namensliste|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|lt=Small Shikinaisha|lt_ja=式内小社|lt_de=Kleine Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi-shiki Divine Registry|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and a [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Old Shrine Rank]] shrine, designating it as a prefectural shrine. Branch shrines exist throughout Kyushu under the names Shijiki, Shishiki, and Shiji, making it the largest among prefectural Shikinaisha. Ancient deity sumo, preserved here, is said to be the precursor to modern [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]].[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 志々伎神社]. 角川日本地名大辞典.JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3. Nasubi History Museum. As of May 18, 2003, the [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Original]{{リンク切れ|date=2022年2月}} archive The Jangara, performed throughout Hirado Island, also spread from this shrine. == Overview == It is said that the shrine was founded around the 5th century, though the exact date remains unknown. The sacred precinct centers on {{ill|Shijiki Mountain|de|Shijiki Berg|ja|志々伎山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijiki Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Shijiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490806}} (with an elevation of 347.2 [[:en:Metre|meter]]) near the southern tip of [[:en:Hirado Island|Hirado Island]]. In ancient times, the upper shrine was located at the mountain summit, the middle shrine on the mid-slope, and the ground shrine (also known as the lower shrine or Hentomiyashiro) was situated in the fishing village of Miyano-ura at the foot, while an offshore shrine was placed on an islet off Miyano-ura. In addition, a bettō-ji known as Enman-ji was established. The enshrined deity is the son of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|de|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|1=Yamato Takeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, and the half-brother of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]. He is said to have followed [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] in the {{ill|Campaigns against the Three Han|de|Drei-Han-Feldzüge|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Campaigns against the Three Han|qq=|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}}, later remaining in the area and being commissioned to guard the western regions, known as {{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|de|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|ja|十城別命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})). The ground shrine is said to have repurposed an armory established by the king, while the offshore shrine is believed to mark his tomb. In the Divine Name List of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engi-shiki]], within [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]], it is listed as one of the four {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}; it is the only shrine of its kind in Nagasaki Prefecture, excluding the Iki and Tsushima regions, and is the oldest known shrine. It has long garnered widespread reverence from the {{ill|Matsuura Faction|de|Matsuura Fraktion|ja|松浦党|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Faction|qq=|1=Matsura-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17564485}} and local fishermen. The current center of worship is the middle shrine. Originally, the shrine was housed in a small structure at the foot of Shijiki Mountain’s summit, but it was relocated to the mid-slope in [[:en:Eiroku|Yōroku]] 2 ([[:en:1559|1559]]). Furthermore, in [[:en:1961|1961]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 36), it was moved to its present location, which occupies the former site of Enman-ji—a bettō-ji abolished in the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|de|Trennung von Shinto und Buddhismus Dekret|ja|神仏分離令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}. The former shrine grounds were designated as a historical site of Nagasaki Prefecture in [[:en:1974|1974]] (Showa 49), under the name "Former Shikinaisha Shijiki Shrine Site." == Footnotes == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{commonscat|Shijiki-jinja}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{Shinto stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Authority control}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[de:Shijiki Shrine]] [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] ==Merged second translation== == Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) == '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志々伎神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Noko Town|ja|野子町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noko Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also written as '''Shishiki Shrine''' or '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志志伎神社 or 志自伎神社). It is listed as a '''small-scale''' {{ill|Shikinai shrine|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinai shrine|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, and under the '''modern shrine Ranking system''', it held the status of a '''former prefectural shrine'''. Several branch shrines bearing the name '''Shijiki''', '''Shishiki''', or '''Shijiki Shrine''' exist throughout Kyushu. As the '''largest of Nagasaki's Shikinaisha''', it has played a significant historical role. The '''sacred sumo ritual''' practiced here is believed to be an early form of '''sumo wrestling'''[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 Shijiki Shrine]. Kadokawa Japan Place Name Encyclopedia, JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3, Nasubi Historical Archive. Archived from [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html the original] on May 18, 2003.. The '''Hirado Jangara Dance''' (平戸のジャンガラ), a local folk performance seen across '''Hirado Island''', is said to have originated from this shrine. == Overview == The founding date of '''Shijiki Shrine''' is uncertain, though some sources suggest it dates back to the '''5th century'''. The shrine’s '''sacred domain''' is centered on '''Mount Shijiki''' (志々伎山), located near the '''southern tip of Hirado Island'''. Traditionally, the shrine was divided into four main sanctuaries: * '''Upper Shrine (Kami-no-Miya)''': Located at the '''summit''' of Mount Shijiki. * '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''': Located '''midway up the mountain'''. * '''Lower Shrine (Chi-no-Miya)''': Located at '''Miyanoura''', a coastal fishing village. * '''Offshore Shrine (Oki-no-Miya)''': Located on '''Okinoshima''', an island off the coast. Additionally, the '''Enman-ji Temple''' (円満寺) historically served as the shrine’s '''bettō-ji''' (shrine-temple complex before the Meiji-era Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''deity enshrined''' here is '''Tokowake-no-Ō''' (十城別王), also known as '''Tokowake-no-Mikoto''' (十城別命), the '''son of Yamato Takeru''' and the '''half-brother of Emperor Chūai'''. He is believed to have '''accompanied Empress Jingū on her conquest of Korea''' and was later '''assigned to defend Western Japan'''. According to legend: * '''Chi-no-Miya''' (Lower Shrine) was originally a '''military weapons storehouse''' established by Tokowake-no-Ō. * '''Oki-no-Miya''' (Offshore Shrine) is said to '''house his tomb'''. Shijiki Shrine is listed in the '''Engishiki''' (延喜式) as '''one of only four Shikinaisha''' in '''Hizen Province'''. Excluding the '''Iki and Tsushima regions''', it is the '''only Engishiki shrine in modern Nagasaki Prefecture'''. Historically, it attracted strong patronage from the '''Matsura clan''' (松浦党) and the surrounding '''maritime communities'''. Today, the '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''' is the primary place of worship. The shrine originally '''stood at the mountain’s peak''', but in '''1559 (Eiroku 2)''', it was moved '''midway down the mountain'''. In '''1961 (Shōwa 36)''', it was '''relocated again''' to the '''former site of Enman-ji Temple''', which had been abolished following the '''Meiji-era Shinbutsu Bunri''' (Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''original shrine site''' was designated a '''Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1974 (Shōwa 49)''' as the '''"Former Site of the Shikinai Shrine Shijiki Shrine"'''. == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] }} [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shikizuki Shrine]] [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirado, Nagasaki]] [[Category:History of Hirado City]] {{Commons category|Shijiki-jinja}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]

As the '''largest of Nagasaki's Shikinaisha''', it has played a significant historical role. The '''sacred sumo ritual''' practiced here is believed to be an early form of '''sumo wrestling'''[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 Shijiki Shrine]. Kadokawa Japan Place Name Encyclopedia, JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3, Nasubi Historical Archive. Archived from [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html the original] on May 18, 2003.. The '''Hirado Jangara Dance''' (平戸のジャンガラ), a local folk performance seen across '''Hirado Island''', is said to have originated from this shrine. == Overview == The founding date of '''Shijiki Shrine''' is uncertain, though some sources suggest it dates back to the '''5th century'''. The shrine’s '''sacred domain''' is centered on '''Mount Shijiki''' (志々伎山), located near the '''southern tip of Hirado Island'''. Traditionally, the shrine was divided into four main sanctuaries: * '''Upper Shrine (Kami-no-Miya)''': Located at the '''summit''' of Mount Shijiki. * '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''': Located '''midway up the mountain'''. * '''Lower Shrine (Chi-no-Miya)''': Located at '''Miyanoura''', a coastal fishing village. * '''Offshore Shrine (Oki-no-Miya)''': Located on '''Okinoshima''', an island off the coast. Additionally, the '''Enman-ji Temple''' (円満寺) historically served as the shrine’s '''bettō-ji''' (shrine-temple complex before the Meiji-era Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''deity enshrined''' here is '''Tokowake-no-Ō''' (十城別王), also known as '''Tokowake-no-Mikoto''' (十城別命), the '''son of Yamato Takeru''' and the '''half-brother of Emperor Chūai'''. He is believed to have '''accompanied Empress Jingū on her conquest of Korea''' and was later '''assigned to defend Western Japan'''. According to legend: * '''Chi-no-Miya''' (Lower Shrine) was originally a '''military weapons storehouse''' established by Tokowake-no-Ō. * '''Oki-no-Miya''' (Offshore Shrine) is said to '''house his tomb'''. Shijiki Shrine is listed in the '''Engishiki''' (延喜式) as '''one of only four Shikinaisha''' in '''Hizen Province'''. Excluding the '''Iki and Tsushima regions''', it is the '''only Engishiki shrine in modern Nagasaki Prefecture'''. Historically, it attracted strong patronage from the '''Matsura clan''' (松浦党) and the surrounding '''maritime communities'''. Today, the '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''' is the primary place of worship. The shrine originally '''stood at the mountain’s peak''', but in '''1559 (Eiroku 2)''', it was moved '''midway down the mountain'''. In '''1961 (Shōwa 36)''', it was '''relocated again''' to the '''former site of Enman-ji Temple''', which had been abolished following the '''Meiji-era Shinbutsu Bunri''' (Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''original shrine site''' was designated a '''Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1974 (Shōwa 49)''' as the '''"Former Site of the Shikinai Shrine Shijiki Shrine"'''. == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] }} [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shikizuki Shrine]] [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirado, Nagasaki]] [[Category:History of Hirado City]] {{Commons category|Shijiki-jinja}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11490808]] Wikidata name: Shijiki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| |Name=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) |image=File:Shijiki-jinja, Hirado 202405-1.jpg |Location=[[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado City]] Nogomachi 251 |Coordinates={{Wiki Coordinates Two-Level DMS|33|10|44.15|N|129|22|08.55|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社)}} |Enshrined deity={{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |Shrine Rank=Shikinaisha (small)
Former Prefectural Shrine |Founded=Unknown |Main Hall= |Alternate name= |Temple sites, etc.= |Annual festival= |Rituals= |Map={{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|marker=place-of-worship}}
{{Right|{{Location map|Japan Nagasaki#Japan|label=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=Location of Shijiki Shrine}}}} |prefecture={{ill|Hirado|qid=Q328601}} |coordinates={{Coord|33.178930555556|N|129.36904166667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Shijiki Shrine''' (Shijikijinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Nogomachi, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado City]] in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also referred to as Shijiki Shrine or Shiji Shrine. It holds the status of a {{ill|Engi-shiki Divine Registry|de|Engi-shiki Göttliche Namensliste|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|lt=Small Shikinaisha|lt_ja=式内小社|lt_de=Kleine Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi-shiki Divine Registry|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and a [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Old Shrine Rank]] shrine, designating it as a prefectural shrine. Branch shrines exist throughout Kyushu under the names Shijiki, Shishiki, and Shiji, making it the largest among prefectural Shikinaisha. Ancient deity sumo, preserved here, is said to be the precursor to modern [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]].[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 志々伎神社]. 角川日本地名大辞典.JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3. Nasubi History Museum. As of May 18, 2003, the [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Original]{{リンク切れ|date=2022年2月}} archive The Jangara, performed throughout Hirado Island, also spread from this shrine. == Overview == It is said that the shrine was founded around the 5th century, though the exact date remains unknown. The sacred precinct centers on {{ill|Shijiki Mountain|de|Shijiki Berg|ja|志々伎山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijiki Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Shijiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490806}} (with an elevation of 347.2 [[:en:Metre|meter]]) near the southern tip of [[:en:Hirado Island|Hirado Island]]. In ancient times, the upper shrine was located at the mountain summit, the middle shrine on the mid-slope, and the ground shrine (also known as the lower shrine or Hentomiyashiro) was situated in the fishing village of Miyano-ura at the foot, while an offshore shrine was placed on an islet off Miyano-ura. In addition, a bettō-ji known as Enman-ji was established. The enshrined deity is the son of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|de|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|1=Yamato Takeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, and the half-brother of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]. He is said to have followed [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] in the {{ill|Campaigns against the Three Han|de|Drei-Han-Feldzüge|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Campaigns against the Three Han|qq=|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}}, later remaining in the area and being commissioned to guard the western regions, known as {{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|de|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|ja|十城別命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})). The ground shrine is said to have repurposed an armory established by the king, while the offshore shrine is believed to mark his tomb. In the Divine Name List of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engi-shiki]], within [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]], it is listed as one of the four {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}; it is the only shrine of its kind in Nagasaki Prefecture, excluding the Iki and Tsushima regions, and is the oldest known shrine. It has long garnered widespread reverence from the {{ill|Matsuura Faction|de|Matsuura Fraktion|ja|松浦党|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Faction|qq=|1=Matsura-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17564485}} and local fishermen. The current center of worship is the middle shrine. Originally, the shrine was housed in a small structure at the foot of Shijiki Mountain’s summit, but it was relocated to the mid-slope in [[:en:Eiroku|Yōroku]] 2 ([[:en:1559|1559]]). Furthermore, in [[:en:1961|1961]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 36), it was moved to its present location, which occupies the former site of Enman-ji—a bettō-ji abolished in the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|de|Trennung von Shinto und Buddhismus Dekret|ja|神仏分離令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}. The former shrine grounds were designated as a historical site of Nagasaki Prefecture in [[:en:1974|1974]] (Showa 49), under the name "Former Shikinaisha Shijiki Shrine Site." == Footnotes == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{commonscat|Shijiki-jinja}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{Shinto stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Authority control}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[de:Shijiki Shrine]] [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] ==Merged second translation== == Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) == '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志々伎神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Noko Town|ja|野子町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noko Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also written as '''Shishiki Shrine''' or '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志志伎神社 or 志自伎神社). It is listed as a '''small-scale''' {{ill|Shikinai shrine|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinai shrine|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, and under the '''modern shrine Ranking system''', it held the status of a '''former prefectural shrine'''. Several branch shrines bearing the name '''Shijiki''', '''Shishiki''', or '''Shijiki Shrine''' exist throughout Kyushu. As the '''largest of Nagasaki's Shikinaisha''', it has played a significant historical role. The '''sacred sumo ritual''' practiced here is believed to be an early form of '''sumo wrestling'''[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 Shijiki Shrine]. Kadokawa Japan Place Name Encyclopedia, JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3, Nasubi Historical Archive. Archived from [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html the original] on May 18, 2003.. The '''Hirado Jangara Dance''' (平戸のジャンガラ), a local folk performance seen across '''Hirado Island''', is said to have originated from this shrine. == Overview == The founding date of '''Shijiki Shrine''' is uncertain, though some sources suggest it dates back to the '''5th century'''. The shrine’s '''sacred domain''' is centered on '''Mount Shijiki''' (志々伎山), located near the '''southern tip of Hirado Island'''. Traditionally, the shrine was divided into four main sanctuaries: * '''Upper Shrine (Kami-no-Miya)''': Located at the '''summit''' of Mount Shijiki. * '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''': Located '''midway up the mountain'''. * '''Lower Shrine (Chi-no-Miya)''': Located at '''Miyanoura''', a coastal fishing village. * '''Offshore Shrine (Oki-no-Miya)''': Located on '''Okinoshima''', an island off the coast. Additionally, the '''Enman-ji Temple''' (円満寺) historically served as the shrine’s '''bettō-ji''' (shrine-temple complex before the Meiji-era Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''deity enshrined''' here is '''Tokowake-no-Ō''' (十城別王), also known as '''Tokowake-no-Mikoto''' (十城別命), the '''son of Yamato Takeru''' and the '''half-brother of Emperor Chūai'''. He is believed to have '''accompanied Empress Jingū on her conquest of Korea''' and was later '''assigned to defend Western Japan'''. According to legend: * '''Chi-no-Miya''' (Lower Shrine) was originally a '''military weapons storehouse''' established by Tokowake-no-Ō. * '''Oki-no-Miya''' (Offshore Shrine) is said to '''house his tomb'''. Shijiki Shrine is listed in the '''Engishiki''' (延喜式) as '''one of only four Shikinaisha''' in '''Hizen Province'''. Excluding the '''Iki and Tsushima regions''', it is the '''only Engishiki shrine in modern Nagasaki Prefecture'''. Historically, it attracted strong patronage from the '''Matsura clan''' (松浦党) and the surrounding '''maritime communities'''. Today, the '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''' is the primary place of worship. The shrine originally '''stood at the mountain’s peak''', but in '''1559 (Eiroku 2)''', it was moved '''midway down the mountain'''. In '''1961 (Shōwa 36)''', it was '''relocated again''' to the '''former site of Enman-ji Temple''', which had been abolished following the '''Meiji-era Shinbutsu Bunri''' (Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''original shrine site''' was designated a '''Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1974 (Shōwa 49)''' as the '''"Former Site of the Shikinai Shrine Shijiki Shrine"'''. == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] }} [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shikizuki Shrine]] [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirado, Nagasaki]] [[Category:History of Hirado City]] {{Commons category|Shijiki-jinja}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]

The current center of worship is the middle shrine. Originally, the shrine was housed in a small structure at the foot of Shijiki Mountain’s summit, but it was relocated to the mid-slope in [[:en:Eiroku|Yōroku]] 2 ([[:en:1559|1559]]). Furthermore, in [[:en:1961|1961]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 36), it was moved to its present location, which occupies the former site of Enman-ji—a bettō-ji abolished in the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|de|Trennung von Shinto und Buddhismus Dekret|ja|神仏分離令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}. The former shrine grounds were designated as a historical site of Nagasaki Prefecture in [[:en:1974|1974]] (Showa 49), under the name "Former Shikinaisha Shijiki Shrine Site." == Footnotes == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{commonscat|Shijiki-jinja}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{Shinto stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Authority control}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[de:Shijiki Shrine]] [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] ==Merged second translation== == Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) == '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志々伎神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Noko Town|ja|野子町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noko Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also written as '''Shishiki Shrine''' or '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志志伎神社 or 志自伎神社). It is listed as a '''small-scale''' {{ill|Shikinai shrine|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinai shrine|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, and under the '''modern shrine Ranking system''', it held the status of a '''former prefectural shrine'''. Several branch shrines bearing the name '''Shijiki''', '''Shishiki''', or '''Shijiki Shrine''' exist throughout Kyushu. As the '''largest of Nagasaki's Shikinaisha''', it has played a significant historical role. The '''sacred sumo ritual''' practiced here is believed to be an early form of '''sumo wrestling'''[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 Shijiki Shrine]. Kadokawa Japan Place Name Encyclopedia, JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3, Nasubi Historical Archive. Archived from [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html the original] on May 18, 2003.. The '''Hirado Jangara Dance''' (平戸のジャンガラ), a local folk performance seen across '''Hirado Island''', is said to have originated from this shrine. == Overview == The founding date of '''Shijiki Shrine''' is uncertain, though some sources suggest it dates back to the '''5th century'''. The shrine’s '''sacred domain''' is centered on '''Mount Shijiki''' (志々伎山), located near the '''southern tip of Hirado Island'''. Traditionally, the shrine was divided into four main sanctuaries: * '''Upper Shrine (Kami-no-Miya)''': Located at the '''summit''' of Mount Shijiki. * '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''': Located '''midway up the mountain'''. * '''Lower Shrine (Chi-no-Miya)''': Located at '''Miyanoura''', a coastal fishing village. * '''Offshore Shrine (Oki-no-Miya)''': Located on '''Okinoshima''', an island off the coast. Additionally, the '''Enman-ji Temple''' (円満寺) historically served as the shrine’s '''bettō-ji''' (shrine-temple complex before the Meiji-era Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''deity enshrined''' here is '''Tokowake-no-Ō''' (十城別王), also known as '''Tokowake-no-Mikoto''' (十城別命), the '''son of Yamato Takeru''' and the '''half-brother of Emperor Chūai'''. He is believed to have '''accompanied Empress Jingū on her conquest of Korea''' and was later '''assigned to defend Western Japan'''. According to legend: * '''Chi-no-Miya''' (Lower Shrine) was originally a '''military weapons storehouse''' established by Tokowake-no-Ō. * '''Oki-no-Miya''' (Offshore Shrine) is said to '''house his tomb'''. Shijiki Shrine is listed in the '''Engishiki''' (延喜式) as '''one of only four Shikinaisha''' in '''Hizen Province'''. Excluding the '''Iki and Tsushima regions''', it is the '''only Engishiki shrine in modern Nagasaki Prefecture'''. Historically, it attracted strong patronage from the '''Matsura clan''' (松浦党) and the surrounding '''maritime communities'''. Today, the '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''' is the primary place of worship. The shrine originally '''stood at the mountain’s peak''', but in '''1559 (Eiroku 2)''', it was moved '''midway down the mountain'''. In '''1961 (Shōwa 36)''', it was '''relocated again''' to the '''former site of Enman-ji Temple''', which had been abolished following the '''Meiji-era Shinbutsu Bunri''' (Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''original shrine site''' was designated a '''Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1974 (Shōwa 49)''' as the '''"Former Site of the Shikinai Shrine Shijiki Shrine"'''. == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] }} [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shikizuki Shrine]] [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirado, Nagasaki]] [[Category:History of Hirado City]] {{Commons category|Shijiki-jinja}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11490808]] Wikidata name: Shijiki Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:18, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| |Name=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) |image=File:Shijiki-jinja, Hirado 202405-1.jpg |Location=[[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]][[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado City]] Nogomachi 251 |Coordinates={{Wiki Coordinates Two-Level DMS|33|10|44.15|N|129|22|08.55|E|region:JP-42_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社)}} |Enshrined deity={{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |Shrine Rank=Shikinaisha (small)
Former Prefectural Shrine |Founded=Unknown |Main Hall= |Alternate name= |Temple sites, etc.= |Annual festival= |Rituals= |Map={{Maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|marker=place-of-worship}}
{{Right|{{Location map|Japan Nagasaki#Japan|label=Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社)|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=Location of Shijiki Shrine}}}} |prefecture={{ill|Hirado|qid=Q328601}} |coordinates={{Coord|33.178930555556|N|129.36904166667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Shijiki Shrine''' (Shijikijinja) is a Shinto shrine located in Nogomachi, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado City]] in [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also referred to as Shijiki Shrine or Shiji Shrine. It holds the status of a {{ill|Engi-shiki Divine Registry|de|Engi-shiki Göttliche Namensliste|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|lt=Small Shikinaisha|lt_ja=式内小社|lt_de=Kleine Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi-shiki Divine Registry|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} and a [[:en:Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|Old Shrine Rank]] shrine, designating it as a prefectural shrine. Branch shrines exist throughout Kyushu under the names Shijiki, Shishiki, and Shiji, making it the largest among prefectural Shikinaisha. Ancient deity sumo, preserved here, is said to be the precursor to modern [[:en:Sumo|Sumo]].[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 志々伎神社]. 角川日本地名大辞典.JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3. Nasubi History Museum. As of May 18, 2003, the [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Original]{{リンク切れ|date=2022年2月}} archive The Jangara, performed throughout Hirado Island, also spread from this shrine. == Overview == It is said that the shrine was founded around the 5th century, though the exact date remains unknown. The sacred precinct centers on {{ill|Shijiki Mountain|de|Shijiki Berg|ja|志々伎山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shijiki Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Shijiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11490806}} (with an elevation of 347.2 [[:en:Metre|meter]]) near the southern tip of [[:en:Hirado Island|Hirado Island]]. In ancient times, the upper shrine was located at the mountain summit, the middle shrine on the mid-slope, and the ground shrine (also known as the lower shrine or Hentomiyashiro) was situated in the fishing village of Miyano-ura at the foot, while an offshore shrine was placed on an islet off Miyano-ura. In addition, a bettō-ji known as Enman-ji was established. The enshrined deity is the son of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|de|Yamato Takeru|ja|日本武尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Takeru|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヤマトタケル|1=Yamato Takeru|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q461258}}, and the half-brother of [[:en:Emperor Chūai|Emperor Chūai]]. He is said to have followed [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] in the {{ill|Campaigns against the Three Han|de|Drei-Han-Feldzüge|ja|三韓征伐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Campaigns against the Three Han|qq=|1=Silla–Wa War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11249435}}, later remaining in the area and being commissioned to guard the western regions, known as {{ill|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|de|Jūjōbetsu-Ō|ja|十城別王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-Ō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|de|Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|ja|十城別命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jūjōbetsu-no-mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})). The ground shrine is said to have repurposed an armory established by the king, while the offshore shrine is believed to mark his tomb. In the Divine Name List of the [[:en:Engishiki|Engi-shiki]], within [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]], it is listed as one of the four {{ill|Shikinaisha|de|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}; it is the only shrine of its kind in Nagasaki Prefecture, excluding the Iki and Tsushima regions, and is the oldest known shrine. It has long garnered widespread reverence from the {{ill|Matsuura Faction|de|Matsuura Fraktion|ja|松浦党|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsuura Faction|qq=|1=Matsura-tō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17564485}} and local fishermen. The current center of worship is the middle shrine. Originally, the shrine was housed in a small structure at the foot of Shijiki Mountain’s summit, but it was relocated to the mid-slope in [[:en:Eiroku|Yōroku]] 2 ([[:en:1559|1559]]). Furthermore, in [[:en:1961|1961]] ([[:en:Shōwa era|Showa]] 36), it was moved to its present location, which occupies the former site of Enman-ji—a bettō-ji abolished in the early Meiji period under the {{ill|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|de|Trennung von Shinto und Buddhismus Dekret|ja|神仏分離令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto-Buddhism Separation Edict|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神仏分離|1=shinbutsu bunri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1970535}}. The former shrine grounds were designated as a historical site of Nagasaki Prefecture in [[:en:1974|1974]] (Showa 49), under the name "Former Shikinaisha Shijiki Shrine Site." == Footnotes == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Notes"}} === References === {{Reflist|2}} == See Also == {{commonscat|Shijiki-jinja}} * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{Shinto stub}} {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Authority control}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[de:Shijiki Shrine]] [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] ==Merged second translation== == Shijiki Shrine (志々伎神社) == '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志々伎神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in {{ill|Noko Town|ja|野子町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noko Town|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[:en:Hirado, Nagasaki|Hirado, Nagasaki]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is also written as '''Shishiki Shrine''' or '''Shijiki Shrine''' (志志伎神社 or 志自伎神社). It is listed as a '''small-scale''' {{ill|Shikinai shrine|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|qq=draft|1=Shikinaisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinai shrine|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}}, and under the '''modern shrine Ranking system''', it held the status of a '''former prefectural shrine'''. Several branch shrines bearing the name '''Shijiki''', '''Shishiki''', or '''Shijiki Shrine''' exist throughout Kyushu. As the '''largest of Nagasaki's Shikinaisha''', it has played a significant historical role. The '''sacred sumo ritual''' practiced here is believed to be an early form of '''sumo wrestling'''[http://jlogos.com/docomosp/word.html?id=7221037 Shijiki Shrine]. Kadokawa Japan Place Name Encyclopedia, JLogos.com“[https://web.archive.org/web/20030518172110/http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html Shijiki Shrine Festival]”. Shijiki Shrine-3, Nasubi Historical Archive. Archived from [http://www53.tok2.com/home/nasubi/history/shjikijinja/shijikijinja3.html the original] on May 18, 2003.. The '''Hirado Jangara Dance''' (平戸のジャンガラ), a local folk performance seen across '''Hirado Island''', is said to have originated from this shrine. == Overview == The founding date of '''Shijiki Shrine''' is uncertain, though some sources suggest it dates back to the '''5th century'''. The shrine’s '''sacred domain''' is centered on '''Mount Shijiki''' (志々伎山), located near the '''southern tip of Hirado Island'''. Traditionally, the shrine was divided into four main sanctuaries: * '''Upper Shrine (Kami-no-Miya)''': Located at the '''summit''' of Mount Shijiki. * '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''': Located '''midway up the mountain'''. * '''Lower Shrine (Chi-no-Miya)''': Located at '''Miyanoura''', a coastal fishing village. * '''Offshore Shrine (Oki-no-Miya)''': Located on '''Okinoshima''', an island off the coast. Additionally, the '''Enman-ji Temple''' (円満寺) historically served as the shrine’s '''bettō-ji''' (shrine-temple complex before the Meiji-era Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''deity enshrined''' here is '''Tokowake-no-Ō''' (十城別王), also known as '''Tokowake-no-Mikoto''' (十城別命), the '''son of Yamato Takeru''' and the '''half-brother of Emperor Chūai'''. He is believed to have '''accompanied Empress Jingū on her conquest of Korea''' and was later '''assigned to defend Western Japan'''. According to legend: * '''Chi-no-Miya''' (Lower Shrine) was originally a '''military weapons storehouse''' established by Tokowake-no-Ō. * '''Oki-no-Miya''' (Offshore Shrine) is said to '''house his tomb'''. Shijiki Shrine is listed in the '''Engishiki''' (延喜式) as '''one of only four Shikinaisha''' in '''Hizen Province'''. Excluding the '''Iki and Tsushima regions''', it is the '''only Engishiki shrine in modern Nagasaki Prefecture'''. Historically, it attracted strong patronage from the '''Matsura clan''' (松浦党) and the surrounding '''maritime communities'''. Today, the '''Middle Shrine (Naka-no-Miya)''' is the primary place of worship. The shrine originally '''stood at the mountain’s peak''', but in '''1559 (Eiroku 2)''', it was moved '''midway down the mountain'''. In '''1961 (Shōwa 36)''', it was '''relocated again''' to the '''former site of Enman-ji Temple''', which had been abolished following the '''Meiji-era Shinbutsu Bunri''' (Shinto-Buddhist separation). The '''original shrine site''' was designated a '''Nagasaki Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1974 (Shōwa 49)''' as the '''"Former Site of the Shikinai Shrine Shijiki Shrine"'''. == References == === Notes === {{Reflist|group="Note"}} === Citations === {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines in Japan|List of Shinto shrines in Japan]] {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{translated page|ja|志々伎神社|version=104096595|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hizen Province]] }} [[ja:%E5%BF%97%E3%80%85%E4%BC%8E%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE]] [[sn:Shikizuki Shrine]] [[ja:志々伎神社]] [[sn:Shijiki Shrine]] [[Category:Buildings in Hirado, Nagasaki]] [[Category:History of Hirado City]] {{Commons category|Shijiki-jinja}} [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]
